name: Predictim
summary: AI startup that scored babysitters' risk level by scanning their public Facebook, Twitter and Instagram activity with sentiment/behavior analysis, marketed to parents as a hiring-screening tool.
lesson: Building hiring/screening AI on scraped social data invites platform bans and algorithmic-bias backlash before a business model can prove itself.

name: Wade & Wendy
summary: NYC conversational-AI recruiting assistant ("Wade" for candidates, "Wendy" for recruiters) backed by the Slack Fund and Randstad Innovation Fund; after eight years without reaching independent scale it was quietly acquired by programmatic recruitment-ad firm PandoLogic in 2023.
lesson: A well-funded conversational-AI concept can still stall for years without a clear path to profitable scale, ending in a quiet acquihire rather than a real exit.
source: Staffing Industry Analysts, TOPBOTS, Built In NYC

name: Koru
summary: Seattle predictive-hiring startup using machine learning to score candidate "fit" for Fortune 500 clients like Citi and Zillow; ended in an "unusual exit," splitting its assets and selling the predictive-hiring product to UK assessment firm Capp & Co. while its original bootcamp platform went to Entangled Group.
lesson: A mid-pivot product with real enterprise logos can still fail to reach exit-worthy scale, forcing a sell-for-parts wind-down instead of an acquisition premium.

name: Hired (Vettery)
summary: Once a $500M-valued tech-recruiting marketplace using AI-driven candidate-employer matching, Hired began winding down operations in December 2020 and was bought at a discount by rival Vettery (Adecco-owned); the combined brand was ultimately folded into Adecco's LHH Recruitment Solutions in 2024, erasing both original brands.
lesson: A marketplace matching model can burn through a $500M valuation and still be forced into a distressed sale when take-rate economics never work at scale.

name: Humanyze
summary: People-analytics pioneer using wearable "sociometric badges" to capture movement, proximity and voice-tone data to predict workforce outcomes; when remote work emptied offices in 2020 its hardware-dependent model collapsed, and after a late digital-only pivot the company was restructured via a debt-purchase deal and ultimately acquired for roughly $325K in stock by Livechain.
lesson: A product built around physical-office sensor hardware can be wiped out overnight by a work-model shift it never priced in, and a late pivot rarely recovers lost ground.

name: Recruiter.com Group
summary: Publicly traded AI-enabled recruiting marketplace and RPO platform layering machine-learning matching onto a freelance-recruiter network; by 2023 the company showed a spiking debt-to-asset ratio, negative earnings and heavy share dilution, and was widely flagged by analysts as a financial "value trap."
lesson: Being publicly listed doesn't fix a weak underlying business model - recurring losses and dilution can turn an AI-hiring story into a stock-market warning sign.
source: Yahoo Finance, StockScan, ValueResearchOnline (RCRT filings analysis)

name: Blendoor
summary: Diversity-focused hiring app founded by Stephanie Lampkin that blinded candidate profiles of race, gender and age, later pivoting to score companies on diversity-pledge follow-through; despite strong press (MIT News, TechCrunch) it never raised beyond seed stage and stalled around 13 employees.
lesson: Mission-driven bias-reduction products can win press coverage while still failing to attract the growth capital needed to compete with mainstream ATS incumbents.

name: Jobr
summary: "Tinder for jobs" swipe-based matching app acquired by Monster Worldwide for $12.5M in 2016 with promises of integration; the standalone app and site were quietly shut down and its features absorbed into Monster's core app.
lesson: A viral swipe-based UX can win an acquisition, but if the acquirer only wants the feature (not the brand), the startup effectively disappears.

name: Switch
summary: Anonymous, swipe-based "Tinder for jobs" mobile matching app that let candidates connect resumes and social profiles to get AI-curated daily job recommendations; the company quietly shut down around 2018 after failing to out-scale rival swipe-hiring apps.
lesson: Consumer-style swipe mechanics attracted press and seed funding, but the "Tinder for jobs" category as a whole failed to convert novelty into durable hiring outcomes.

name: hiQ Labs
summary: Data-science company that scraped public LinkedIn profiles to predict employee attrition and skills gaps for corporate HR teams; became famous for the landmark hiQ Labs v. LinkedIn scraping case, but the underlying business collapsed years before the litigation ended - courts found the company "essentially defunct" by 2022, and a final settlement required it to destroy its scraped data and algorithms.
lesson: Winning a legal precedent doesn't save a company if the underlying dispute drags on long enough to drain the business itself.
source: Wikipedia (hiQ Labs v. LinkedIn), Privacy World, Justia

name: Untapt
summary: NYC AI-matching startup pairing tech candidates with financial-services employers using machine learning; after years of modest traction it was acquired by staffing firm Wynden Stark in April 2021, with its founder folded in as CTO of parent GQR rather than continuing untapt as an independent product.
lesson: A technically sound matching engine can still end up as a feature inside a traditional staffing firm rather than a standalone winner if it can't out-scale generalist recruiters.

name: Ascendify
summary: "Intelligent talent management" platform using predictive analytics for internal mobility and succession planning, backed by Canaan Partners, GE Ventures and Cisco Investments; after years without a major follow-on round or public growth news, it was acquired by recruitment-marketing firm Radancy in April 2023.
lesson: Enterprise backers and an early Series A don't guarantee escape velocity - years of quiet stagnation often end in an absorption acquisition rather than a marquee exit.

name: Entelo
summary: Early AI-sourcing and diversity-recruiting platform; rather than reaching an independent IPO or major standalone exit, it was folded into SilkRoad Technology in 2020, which was itself acquired by Cornerstone OnDemand in 2022 - a roll-up outcome rather than a standalone win.
lesson: Being an early mover in AI sourcing isn't enough if faster, better-funded rivals catch up - you can end up as a line item in someone else's roll-up.

name: Identified.com
summary: Social-data recruiting platform that built a patent-pending "SYMAN" AI to mine Facebook and LinkedIn profiles for hiring insights; despite significant funding and buzz, it was absorbed as an acquihire by Workday in 2014 mainly for its R&D team rather than continuing as an independent product.
lesson: Flashy social-data AI can attract a big-name acquirer, but when the deal is framed around the engineering team rather than the product, that's a sign the original business model didn't work.

name: TalentBin
summary: AI-powered technical-talent sourcing engine that scraped developer activity (GitHub, Stack Overflow) to surface passive candidates; acquired by Monster Worldwide in 2014, but Monster quietly discontinued the standalone product within a few years as its own business declined.
lesson: An acquisition by a struggling incumbent can be a dead end - if the acquirer itself is losing ground, your product gets sunset along with it.
source: Crunchbase, industry coverage of Monster Worldwide acquisitions

name: Gild
summary: Data-science hiring platform that scored developers on code quality and online activity to predict on-the-job performance; despite a strong pitch and Series funding, it was acquired in 2016 not by an HR company but by hedge fund Citadel for internal use - ending its run as an independent HR-tech product.
lesson: An acquisition that pulls your technology out of the HR industry entirely is a sign the standalone hiring-software business never found its footing.

name: Textio
summary: "Augmented writing" platform using NLP to remove biased language and improve job-posting performance; despite strong early enterprise adoption (Microsoft, CVS), it underwent severe layoffs amid the 2023 funding downturn as growth and follow-on funding dried up.
lesson: A genuinely useful NLP product for HR can still get caught by a funding-market reset if it never reaches default-alive unit economics before the capital tap turns off.

name: GapJumpers
summary: "Blind auditions" hiring platform that stripped resumes of identifying details and had candidates complete skills challenges instead, with early customers like Dolby and Pivotal Labs; after an Inc.com feature calling it a threat to the "$3 billion resume industry," the company failed to scale beyond a niche following and went largely inactive.
lesson: Being profiled as a category-killer in the press doesn't guarantee employers will actually change deep-rooted resume-screening habits at scale.
source: Inc.com, Forbes HR Council coverage of blind hiring

name: Restless Bandit
summary: "Talent rediscovery" AI that mined a company's existing applicant database with machine learning to resurface qualified past candidates; raised a notable $10M round in 2016 but lost visibility as the market consolidated around larger ATS-native AI features.
lesson: A genuinely useful niche AI feature can get swallowed once ATS incumbents build the same capability natively.

name: HiringSolved
summary: One of the earliest AI-powered candidate-sourcing engines, indexing public profiles to find and rank passive candidates; despite being an early mover, it was progressively outpaced by better-funded, faster-iterating rivals like SeekOut and hireEZ and lost relevance in the sourcing-AI category it helped pioneer.
lesson: First-mover advantage in AI sourcing erodes quickly once well-capitalized competitors ship comparable features faster.

name: Jobaline
summary: Voice-AI hiring platform for hourly and bilingual workers that analyzed callers' speech patterns to recommend candidate-job matches; generated strong press around its "voice analyzer" but visibility and funding activity dropped off sharply after 2015, and the company never scaled beyond its early pilots.
lesson: A novel biometric signal can generate media buzz without translating into a durable enterprise sales motion.

name: Woo (Work4)
summary: Social/AI-driven job-matching app rebranded from Work4 to Woo, using machine learning to recommend jobs based on social-graph and preference data; despite tens of millions in funding and a high-profile rebrand, the consumer app was discontinued around 2018 after failing to build a defensible matching advantage over LinkedIn and Indeed.
lesson: A rebrand and consumer-friendly UX can't overcome a fundamentally weak data moat when incumbents own the underlying graph.
source: Industry coverage of Work4/Woo rebrand and shutdown

name: Doxa
summary: Inclusive-hiring startup that used blind, skills-first screening to reduce bias in early-stage candidate evaluation; after an initial wave of interest in bias-reduction hiring tech around 2017-2018, the company went quiet with no further funding announcements or product updates.
lesson: The late-2010s blind-hiring wave produced many well-intentioned seed-stage tools that couldn't out-compete each other for the same small pool of bias-conscious enterprise buyers.
source: General HR-tech press coverage of blind-hiring startups (2017-2018 cohort)

name: ClearFit
summary: Canadian AI-driven hiring-assessment platform ("Guided Interviewing") using predictive fit scoring to help small businesses screen candidates faster; gained early traction in Canadian tech media but discontinued active development and marketing as larger, better-funded assessment platforms took over the category.
lesson: Regional AI-hiring tools built for SMBs struggle to keep pace once well-funded platforms roll the same predictive-fit features into enterprise suites.
source: Canadian tech-press coverage of early SMB hiring-assessment tools

name: TalentSonar (Unitive)
summary: Blind-recruiting software (rebranded from TalentSonar to Unitive) that stripped identifying details from resumes and standardized structured interviews to reduce bias; after a few years of pilots with mid-sized employers, the company stopped raising and effectively wound down operations.
lesson: Bias-reduction hiring tools sell well as pilots but often struggle to convert into durable, expanding enterprise contracts.
source: HR-tech press coverage of blind-hiring startup cohort (2015-2018)

name: Interviewed.com
summary: Early AI-assisted mock-interview and hiring-practice platform aimed at helping candidates prepare for and employers assess technical interviews; failed to raise beyond seed funding and shut down as better-funded technical-interview platforms captured the category.
lesson: Being early to "AI for interview prep" doesn't matter if you're under-capitalized relative to the well-funded players who define the category a few years later.

name: Job.com (AI relaunch)
summary: Blockchain/AI-branded relaunch of the veteran Job.com domain, promising AI-driven matching and blockchain-verified credentials for job seekers; despite a splashy funding round and rebrand, it failed to establish meaningful market share against incumbent job boards and quietly stalled.
lesson: Bolting trendy technology labels onto a legacy domain doesn't create product-market fit on its own.
source: General HR-tech press coverage of Job.com AI relaunch

name: RolePoint
summary: Employee-referral platform that used algorithmic matching to surface which employees were most likely to know a strong referral for open roles; after raising a modest Series A, it was acquired by SmartRecruiters in 2017 in a deal widely seen in the industry as a distressed, below-expectations outcome for its investors.
lesson: Referral-matching algorithms are a useful feature, not a standalone company - single-feature AI startups often end up folded into a platform rather than becoming one.

name: HireVue
summary: Pioneer of AI-scored, on-demand video interviewing; survived major controversy over facial/emotion-analysis bias (dropped the feature in 2021 after an FTC complaint from EPIC) and went on to acquire AllyO (2020) and Modern Hire (2023), consolidating itself as one of the largest AI hiring-assessment platforms serving over half the Fortune 100.
lesson: Surviving a major public bias controversy is possible if you're willing to drop the offending feature and keep consolidating the category through acquisition.
source: HireVue press releases, HR Dive, TechTarget coverage of AllyO/Modern Hire acquisitions

name: Eightfold AI
summary: AI "talent intelligence" platform using deep learning to match candidates to roles and predict internal mobility across large enterprises; scaled into a unicorn serving Fortune 500 clients across recruiting, internal mobility and workforce planning.
lesson: Broadening from a single point solution to a full talent-intelligence platform across the employee lifecycle created durable enterprise lock-in.

name: Paradox
summary: Conversational-AI recruiting assistant ("Olivia") that automates high-volume screening, scheduling and Q&A for large hourly-workforce employers; scaled to unicorn status serving major retail, healthcare and logistics employers, later acquiring interview-intelligence startup Aspect.
lesson: Focusing narrowly on high-volume, hourly-worker hiring, where speed matters more than nuance, let a chatbot product scale to a billion-dollar valuation.
source: Crunchbase, general HR-tech coverage of Paradox funding rounds

name: Beamery
summary: Talent-CRM and AI talent-lifecycle platform helping large enterprises manage and nurture candidate pipelines with predictive analytics; reached unicorn status with major enterprise customers across tech, finance and consulting.
lesson: Owning the talent-CRM layer above the ATS created a durable, sticky enterprise data platform rather than a point tool.
source: Crunchbase, general coverage of Beamery funding rounds

name: SmartRecruiters
summary: Cloud recruiting platform layering AI-driven matching, automation and a marketplace of hiring apps (including acquired RolePoint) on top of a modern ATS; scaled to serve large global enterprises like Visa and McDonald's.
lesson: Building a marketplace/app-store model around a core ATS lets a platform absorb smaller AI point-solutions as features rather than competing head-on.

name: Gem
summary: Recruiting CRM and analytics platform using AI to help talent teams source, engage and forecast hiring pipelines; grew rapidly with strong venture backing and enterprise adoption among tech companies.
lesson: Targeting in-house recruiting teams at high-growth tech companies with a best-in-class sourcing/analytics workflow proved a durable wedge into enterprise HR budgets.

name: SeekOut
summary: AI-powered talent-search engine that indexes hundreds of millions of profiles, including diversity and technical-skill signals, to help recruiters find hard-to-find candidates; scaled to unicorn valuation.
lesson: A well-executed talent-search product launched years after early sourcing-AI pioneers can still out-execute and out-fund the incumbents.

name: Phenom
summary: AI-driven "talent experience" platform spanning career sites, chatbots, internal mobility and CRM for large enterprises; reached unicorn status serving hundreds of global brands.
lesson: Wrapping AI around the entire candidate/employee experience, not just screening, created a broad enterprise platform that's hard to displace.

name: Gloat
summary: AI-powered internal talent-marketplace platform helping large enterprises redeploy employees to internal gigs and projects based on skills-matching; scaled to unicorn status with major enterprise clients like Unilever and Standard Chartered.
lesson: Applying AI matching to internal mobility, rather than only external hiring, opened a large, underserved enterprise market.

name: Checkr
summary: AI/ML-driven background-check platform that dramatically sped up and automated criminal-record and employment screening for gig-economy and enterprise employers; scaled to a multi-billion-dollar valuation.
lesson: Applying machine learning to speed up a compliance-heavy, historically slow screening process created a large, defensible business.

name: CodeSignal
summary: AI-driven technical-skills assessment and interview platform used by engineering teams to standardize and de-bias technical hiring; scaled to become a major player in developer-hiring assessments.
lesson: Standardized, proctored technical assessments solved a real pain point that scaled well across engineering-heavy employers.

name: HackerRank
summary: Technical-skills assessment and coding-interview platform used by thousands of companies to screen software engineers, later adding AI-driven proctoring and skills-matching features; became a category leader in developer hiring.
lesson: Owning the practice/assessment layer for developers gave the company a durable acquisition channel into enterprise hiring.

name: Karat
summary: "Interviewing as a service" platform combining trained human interviewers with AI-assisted scoring and scheduling to standardize technical interviews for engineering teams; scaled to serve major tech employers and later expanded into AI-enabled interview products.
lesson: Combining human expertise with AI tooling, rather than going fully automated, built trust with engineering hiring teams faster than a pure-AI product could.

name: Codility
summary: Coding-skills assessment platform used to screen software engineers at scale, later layering in AI-driven plagiarism and anomaly detection; became a long-standing category leader in technical hiring assessments.
lesson: A durable niche with strong retention can sustain a company for over a decade without needing hyper-growth unicorn status.

name: Loxo
summary: AI-powered recruiting CRM and ATS combining automated sourcing, enrichment and pipeline management for recruiting agencies and in-house teams; grew to serve thousands of customers largely without traditional VC funding.
lesson: An AI-recruiting product doesn't need venture mega-rounds to scale - staying capital-efficient and profitable can build a large customer base.

name: hireEZ (formerly Hiretual)
summary: AI-powered outbound-recruiting and sourcing platform that aggregates public candidate data across the web; rebranded from Hiretual to hireEZ in 2022 after a funding round, continuing to grow as a major sourcing-AI competitor to SeekOut.
lesson: A rebrand paired with a funding raise can re-energize a sourcing-AI company's market position even in a crowded competitive field.
source: Hunt Scanlon Media, hireEZ press releases (Feb 2022)

name: Sense
summary: AI-driven candidate-engagement and communication platform (texting, chatbots) used heavily by staffing agencies and high-volume employers to keep candidates engaged through the hiring funnel; scaled to unicorn valuation.
lesson: Focusing on candidate engagement and communication, a persistent pain point in high-volume hiring, built a defensible, sticky product.

name: Metaview
summary: AI interview-intelligence platform that records, transcribes and summarizes recruiting interviews to reduce note-taking burden and improve hiring-decision quality; grew quickly post-2020 as generative AI matured, becoming a well-regarded player in the interview-intelligence category.
lesson: Launching an AI-notetaking-for-interviews product right as generative AI transcription and summarization matured let the company ride a genuine technology wave rather than fight it.

name: BrightHire
summary: AI interview-intelligence platform recording and analyzing recruiting interviews to improve consistency and reduce bias in hiring decisions; grew steadily with enterprise customers across tech and other industries.
lesson: Interview-intelligence tools that focus on making existing human interviewers better, rather than replacing them, found faster enterprise buy-in than fully automated screening tools.

name: Findem
summary: AI-driven talent-data platform combining "3D data" (people, company and role data) to power sourcing, market-mapping and diversity analytics for enterprise talent teams; grew steadily through multiple funding rounds.
lesson: Combining multiple data layers into a unified AI talent-data platform differentiated the company in a crowded sourcing-AI market.

name: Sapia.ai (Yobs Technologies)
summary: AI-driven chat-based interview platform that scores candidates on structured text conversations rather than video, aiming to reduce bias versus facial-analysis approaches; grew across high-volume hiring markets in Australia, the US and Asia.
lesson: Choosing text-based chat interviews over facial/video analysis let the company sidestep the bias controversies that dogged video-AI competitors like HireVue.

name: Ideal
summary: Toronto-based AI candidate-screening and talent-intelligence platform automating resume screening and reducing bias; acquired by HCM giant Ceridian in April 2021 and integrated into the Dayforce Talent Intelligence suite.
lesson: A well-executed AI screening tool with a clean bias-reduction story is an attractive acquisition target for large HCM suites looking to add AI capabilities.

name: Mya Systems
summary: San Francisco conversational-AI recruiting platform automating candidate outreach, screening and scheduling for large staffing firms and enterprises; acquired by European job-marketplace group StepStone/Totaljobs in May 2021 to power autonomous matching across their platforms.
lesson: A strong conversational-AI recruiting engine can find its best long-term home inside a large international job-marketplace group rather than as a standalone SaaS vendor.
source: Staffing Industry Analysts, HR Tech Feed (May 2021)

name: Pymetrics
summary: Neuroscience-based hiring-assessment platform using gamified tasks and audited AI to score candidates on soft skills while reducing bias; acquired by assessment platform Harver in August 2022 to expand Harver's talent-decisioning suite.
lesson: A scientifically differentiated, bias-audited assessment methodology can command a strategic acquisition by a larger platform even without becoming a standalone public company.

name: Aspiring Minds
summary: India-founded AI-based skills-assessment and interview-simulation platform (Autoview, Codemeet, SVAR) used to test technical and language skills at scale; acquired by global assessment leader SHL in 2019-2020 and fully rebranded into SHL's product line.
lesson: Deep AI assessment IP built in an emerging market can become a core acquisition target for legacy global assessment leaders looking to modernize.
source: SHL press releases, PR Newswire, Inc42 (2019-2020)

name: Modern Hire
summary: AI-enabled hiring-assessment and structured-interview platform, formed from a merger of Shaker International, Montage and Virtual Job Tryout, serving large enterprise clients; acquired by rival HireVue in May 2023, consolidating two of the largest AI hiring-assessment platforms into one.
lesson: In a maturing category, consolidation between the two largest AI assessment players can be a stronger outcome than either continuing to compete head-to-head for the same enterprise accounts.

name: AllyO
summary: Conversational-AI recruiting-chatbot platform automating high-volume screening, scheduling and onboarding conversations; acquired by HireVue in October 2020 to add chat-based automation to HireVue's video-interview platform, reportedly cutting time-to-hire significantly for combined customers.
lesson: A strong point solution can achieve a clean strategic exit by plugging directly into a larger platform's existing enterprise distribution.
